narrative:
Mark Pocan is that rare congressional specimen: a small business owner who actually joined a union. The Madison Democrat owns Budget Signs & Specialties, a printing company, and is a dues-paying member of the AFL-CIO — which makes him one of the few members of Congress who can talk about working people without sounding like an anthropologist describing a distant civilization. As chair emeritus of the Congressional Progressive Caucus, Pocan has positioned himself as the House's progressive conscience, and his voting record largely backs it up.

On the 80% Bill's planks, Pocan is one of the closer things to a true believer you'll find in Congress. He's called banning congressional stock trading a "no-brainer" and criticized weaker proposals as merely a "stock discouragement act" rather than an actual ban — supporting the stronger "Restore Trust in Congress Act" that would prohibit all congressional stock ownership outright. He voted for both articles of impeachment against Trump, voted to withdraw troops from Syria, and has been a vocal advocate for Medicare drug negotiation and paid family leave. His February 2025 introduction of the ELON MUSK Act — the "Eliminating Looting of Our Nation by Mitigating Unethical State Kleptocracy" Act — targeting Elon Musk's conflicts of interest as a special government employee with over $20 billion in federal contracts, showed a flair for the theatrical that the 80% Bill's messaging could use.

But here's where the applause track fades. Pocan represents Wisconsin's 2nd district, anchored by Madison — a city so progressive that the local Whole Foods probably has a union. He's been in Congress since 2013, before that in the state assembly for 14 years, and faces roughly the same electoral pressure as a tenured professor. His district is so safe that his general election opponents are essentially performing community theater. This comfort has allowed Pocan to be loud on progressive causes without ever having to make the hard compromises that would test his convictions.

Labor unions have donated tens of thousands to his campaigns, which is perfectly legal but worth noting when assessing his independence. Pocan's top donors are unions, not corporations — a meaningful distinction from most of his colleagues — but it still raises the question of whether any member can be truly independent when their funding depends on organized constituencies.

Pocan talks a good game on Pentagon accountability, dark money, and corporate PAC bans. The question is whether the Progressive Caucus's chair emeritus is pushing these issues with the urgency they deserve or simply issuing press releases from the comfort of a safe seat. Madison deserves a representative who fights like his job depends on it — even when it doesn't.

Scott Peters has represented San Diego in Congress since 2013, currently serving California's 50th district. A former environmental lawyer, city council president, and port commissioner, Peters arrived in Washington with a resume that screamed good-government pragmatist [1]. He sits on the Energy and Commerce Committee, chairs the Climate Task Force for the Sustainable Energy and Environment Coalition, and has cultivated a brand as a solutions-oriented moderate who transcends partisan gridlock [2]. He is also, with a family net worth estimated between $59 million and $112 million, one of the wealthiest members of Congress -- a fact that becomes relevant approximately every time he votes [3][4].

Peters checks some boxes on the 80% Bill. He voted for the For the People Act, which included provisions to end gerrymandering and expand voting access [5]. He has called for a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, publicly urging Congress to address unlimited special interest spending [6]. He supported the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained climate provisions he championed and, notably, the Medicare drug price negotiation framework that he personally shaped after opposing the original, stronger version [7]. On climate and energy, his record is legitimately strong -- he co-led the Energy Innovation and Carbon Dividend Act, introduced methane emissions legislation, and fought offshore drilling expansion [2]. He voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq AUMF alongside the House majority in 2021 [8].

But the drug pricing saga is where Peters' record transforms from complicated to damning. In 2021, he emerged as one of the leading Democratic opponents of H.R. 3, Nancy Pelosi's signature bill to let Medicare negotiate drug prices -- a bill supported by roughly 80% of Americans. Peters organized a cadre of moderate Democrats to challenge the measure, arguing it would stifle pharmaceutical innovation [9]. The timing of what happened next was exquisite: the very day after Peters authored a letter opposing the bill, pharmaceutical industry executives flooded his campaign with contributions, including $5,800 from Pfizer's CEO, $5,000 from Eli Lilly's CEO, $2,900 from Merck's CEO, and $2,900 from Bristol Myers Squibb's CEO, plus $1,000 each from PhRMA officials [10]. Over his career, pharmaceutical PACs and employees have donated $860,465 to his campaigns, making it his second-largest industry donor [11]. He was the House's top recipient of pharmaceutical industry money in the 2022 cycle [11]. Peters eventually helped craft the scaled-back compromise that made it into the Inflation Reduction Act, which allows Medicare to negotiate prices on far fewer drugs than the original bill would have [7]. He then took a victory lap, claiming credit for "lowering prescription drug prices" -- which is technically true in the way that putting a band-aid on a compound fracture is technically first aid. More recently, Peters has co-sponsored legislation that would further limit regulators' ability to negotiate drug prices, effectively eroding the very reforms he claimed credit for [12].

Follow the money across his career and the pattern is consistent. Peters' top industries include pharmaceuticals and health products, securities and investment, and real estate [13]. When asked in 2016 whether he would disavow PAC support, his answer was refreshingly honest: "I'm not about to unilaterally disarm," he said, while acknowledging "we have a system that's corrupt" [6]. It is the kind of candor that almost makes you respect the hustle. His wife, Lynn Gorguze, is CEO of Cameron Holdings, a private equity firm, placing the Peters household squarely in the financial elite whose interests intersect with carried interest loopholes, capital gains preferences, and the broader tax architecture the 80% Bill seeks to reform [4].

Peters is the archetype of the donor-compatible Democrat: fluent in reform language, genuinely committed on issues where his donors have no skin in the game, and reliably aligned with industry when the checks clear. He will fight climate change all day long -- San Diego voters demand it and the energy industry has largely pivoted -- but ask him to let Medicare negotiate the price of insulin the way every other developed nation does, and suddenly he discovers the virtues of pharmaceutical innovation. The 80% Bill does not ask representatives to be perfect; it asks them to side with the supermajority over the donor class. On the issue where those two interests most clearly diverged, Peters chose the donors, cashed their checks the next morning, and called it leadership.

Gary Palmer of Alabama's 6th Congressional District is, by his own telling, a man of deep conviction — or at least a man who prays deeply before deciding which convictions to abandon. A co-founder and 25-year president of the Alabama Policy Institute, a conservative think tank with ties to the Koch-funded State Policy Network and ALEC, Palmer arrived in Washington in 2015 not as a traditional politician but as an ideological architect[1][2][3]. He served as chair of the House Republican Policy Committee from 2019 to 2025, shaping what the Republican Party officially believes, at least on paper[4]. The American Conservative Union gave him a 97% lifetime rating[5].

On the 80% Bill's core democratic reforms, Palmer is a reliable obstacle. He has not cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act, has not backed a ban on corporate PACs, and has not moved to close stock-trading loopholes[7][8]. He did vote to release the Epstein files with apparent sincerity — one of the few moments his vote aligned with what most Americans actually want[9]. He also cosponsored term limits legislation, technically fulfilling the letter of a pledge he made in 2014. The spirit of that pledge, however, proved more negotiable: Palmer publicly promised Alabama voters he would serve no more than five terms, then ran for a sixth, explaining that he had prayed about it and received divine clarity that he should continue[10][11]. "I didn't pray about it" when he made the promise, he acknowledged — a confession that raises questions about his earlier legislative output.

On economic policy, the contradictions multiply. Palmer spent years running a think tank dedicated to "free markets" and fiscal discipline, then voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act of 2025 — a reconciliation package projected to add trillions to the deficit while slashing Medicaid[12]. He defended the bill insisting the tax cuts were "going to go to people who earn less than $100,000 a year," while the Center for American Progress documented the largest Medicaid cuts in history embedded in the same legislation[12][13]. He voted to repeal the Affordable Care Act in 2017 and opposed the Inflation Reduction Act, meaning he opposed Medicare's newly won authority to negotiate drug prices[14][15]. The 80% Bill calls for taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing tax loopholes, and funding Social Security. Palmer, in a decade in Congress, has sponsored zero enacted laws on any of those fronts[6].

Follow the money and the picture clarifies. Palmer's career donors include agribusiness, forestry, real estate, and the oil and gas industry[16]. He sits on the House Energy and Commerce Committee, where he chairs the Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations, and where he has introduced resolutions to block rules on natural gas appliances and pushed back on renewable energy regulations[17][18]. His former think tank's activities "arguably cross the line into lobbying," according to The Guardian — a fitting origin story for a congressman whose greatest legislative accomplishment is building an ideological apparatus that others then use to lobby him[3]. Gary Palmer will tell you the government spends too much, then vote for a bill that adds to the deficit. He will tell you he believes in term limits, then ask God for a sixth term. He will chair the committee on oversight and spend a decade overseeing nothing that inconveniences his funders.

Stacey Plaskett is the delegate from the U.S. Virgin Islands, a Democrat now in her sixth term, and a woman whose resume reads like it was designed by an algorithm optimizing for "most interesting career trajectory in Congress." She served as an assistant district attorney in the Bronx, prosecuting hundreds of cases including narcotics work, then was appointed by President George W. Bush to the Department of Justice's Civil Division, where she served as counsel to the assistant attorney general and acting deputy assistant attorney general [1]. She switched parties in 2008, explaining that the Democratic Party was "a better place to have new ideas heard" [2] -- a sentence that has aged like milk left in the Pacific sun.

Plaskett made history in 2021 as the first non-voting delegate ever to serve as a House impeachment manager, presenting video evidence during the second impeachment trial of Donald Trump [3]. The irony of a representative who cannot vote on legislation being chosen to prosecute the case for removing a president was apparently lost on no one except the people who designed the system. She sits on the Ways and Means Committee -- the first territorial delegate and fourth Black woman to do so -- along with the Budget Committee and the Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence [4].

Her campaign finance picture is more colorful than a Caribbean sunset. OpenSecrets data shows roughly half her funding comes from PACs (49%) and half from large individual donors (46%), with a negligible 4% from small donors [5]. Her top contributing industry is lawyers and law firms ($47,841), with Bloomberg LP chipping in $23,100 [6]. But the real headline is Jeffrey Epstein: between 2014 and 2020, Epstein and his affiliates donated over $30,000 to her campaigns. Unsealed documents revealed she personally solicited Epstein's attendance at a Bloomberg fundraiser in 2018 [7]. She returned the money in 2019 after his arrest. A subsequent lawsuit by six Epstein victims alleging Plaskett received financial and political support in exchange for approving over $300 million in tax breaks for Epstein's companies was voluntarily dismissed with prejudice in August 2025 [8].

On the 80% Bill's twenty articles, Plaskett's non-voting status means she cannot cast a decisive floor vote on any of them. Her Ways and Means seat gives her real influence on tax-related provisions -- carried interest, billionaire minimum taxes, Social Security funding -- but that influence comes with an asterisk the size of St. Croix.

Jimmy Panetta is a Democratic congressman representing California's 19th district — the Monterey Bay and Central Coast region — and yes, he is exactly the Panetta you think he is. The son of Leon Panetta, who served as CIA Director, Secretary of Defense, White House Chief of Staff, and congressman from the same stretch of California coastline, Jimmy inherited both his father's seat and his Rolodex [1]. Before Congress, he served as a Navy intelligence officer in Afghanistan and as a Monterey County deputy district attorney. First elected in 2016, he now sits on the Ways and Means, Agriculture, and Armed Services committees — a trifecta that positions him at the intersection of tax policy, farm country, and the defense establishment [2].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Panetta has some genuine wins. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — the first time the government could bargain with pharmaceutical companies — and has touted the law's $2,000 annual out-of-pocket cap and $35 insulin limit for seniors [3][4]. He voted for the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which included body camera mandates and accountability reforms [5]. On protecting U.S. farmland, he introduced bipartisan legislation to give the Committee on Foreign Investment new jurisdiction over agricultural land purchases by foreign adversaries [6], and he championed the Preserving Family Farms Act to ease estate tax burdens on family operations [7]. He recently cosponsored the MEME Act and Stop TRUMP in Crypto Act to ban federal officials from profiting off crypto schemes [8].

But the silences are telling. Panetta has not signed onto the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act, has not championed term limits (his father held the same seat for sixteen years before passing it along), and has been quiet on the DISCLOSE Act, Citizens United, and dark money reform. He has not pushed for AUMF repeal despite his Armed Services Committee seat and military background — a missed opportunity that his father's Pentagon tenure might help explain. On paid family leave, right to repair, junk fees, and the Epstein files, his record is similarly sparse, the positions of a congressman who picks his battles carefully and leaves the populist grandstanding to others.

Then there is the money. In the 2023-2024 cycle, a staggering 52.79% of Panetta's campaign funds came from PACs, with large individual contributions making up another 43.80% and small donors contributing a meager 3.41% [9]. His most notable benefactor was AIPAC and its affiliated donors, who poured more than $250,000 into his campaign — making him the third-highest recipient of AIPAC-affiliated contributions among California congressional incumbents [10]. When more than half your funding comes from PACs and your small-dollar donors barely register, it becomes harder to argue that you are a tribune of the people rather than a reliable vote for the interests that fund you.

Panetta is not a villain in the 80% Bill framework — his votes on drug pricing and police reform are real, and his work on farmland protection is substantive. But he is a case study in comfortable incumbency: a legacy congressman with a famous last name, a defense-intelligence pedigree, and a donor base that leans heavily institutional. The bills he champions tend to be the ones that do not threaten his coalition. The ones he avoids — stock trading bans, dark money reform, term limits — are the ones that would.

Chris Pappas is that rarest of congressional creatures: a guy who actually ran a business before he started telling other people how to run theirs. As fourth-generation co-owner of the Puritan Backroom Restaurant in Manchester—a 106-year-old institution with over 230 employees—Pappas arrived in Congress in 2019 knowing what a payroll looks like, which already puts him ahead of roughly 90 percent of his colleagues [1]. He also made history as the first openly gay man to represent New Hampshire in Congress [2].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Pappas lands in interesting territory. He is among the most independent Democrats in the House, breaking with his party more than 97 percent of other Democrats according to CQ Roll Call's Vote Studies, making him the eighth most likely Democrat to cross the aisle [3]. That independence shows up in a 0.5 percent missed-vote rate—better than the median for all sitting representatives [4]. He has championed veterans' causes from his seat on the Veterans' Affairs Committee, passing bipartisan legislation to expand VA emergency care unanimously [5], and sits on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee where he focuses on highways, water resources, and the Coast Guard [4].

Pappas voted to lower everyday costs for New Hampshire families [6] and ran on a platform that included nationwide paid family leave and a $12 minimum wage [7]. He cosponsored federal right-to-repair legislation that received broad bipartisan support [8] and introduced a bill with Senator Hassan to improve child care access for working families [9]. He has called for expanded early voting and no-excuse vote-by-mail options [7].

Where Pappas falls short is on the bills that would truly rattle the donor class. He has not taken a public position on banning congressional stock trading, auditing the Pentagon, or imposing a lifetime lobbying ban—three planks that poll above 80 percent nationally but apparently poll poorly with campaign treasurers. His Victory Fund raised $242,800 in 2024 [10], and his top industry donors include air transport unions and leadership PAC money channeled through 'Putting New Hampshire First' [10]. He is a pragmatist in a party that increasingly demands purity, which means he actually gets things done—but whether those things include structural anti-corruption reform remains an open question.

Ayanna Pressley has represented Massachusetts's 7th Congressional District since 2019, when she unseated ten-term incumbent Michael Capuano in a primary that shocked the Democratic establishment. A member of the progressive "Squad," Pressley serves on the House Financial Services Committee and the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform [1]. Her district, anchored in Boston, is the most diverse in New England, and Pressley has built a brand around the idea that the people closest to the pain should be closest to the power. It is a compelling motto, and on the evidence of her legislative record, she appears to mean it.

On the 80% Bill's core issues, Pressley's record is remarkably aligned. She has cosponsored legislation to ban members of Congress from trading individual stocks, including a bill targeting defense stocks specifically [2]. She has led efforts to establish universal paid family and medical leave, calling it "a matter of public health, racial, gender, economic and disability justice" [3]. She supports expanding Social Security and Medicare rather than cutting them, and has pushed for housing affordability through the Congressional Progressive Caucus, where she co-chaired a hearing demanding that Congress treat housing as a human right [4]. On campaign finance, Pressley refuses corporate PAC donations for both her congressional committee and her leadership PAC, the Power of Us PAC [5].

The financial picture is refreshingly straightforward for Washington. Pressley's campaign raised just over $1 million in the 2024 cycle, a modest sum by congressional standards, and her refusal of corporate PAC money means her donor base skews heavily toward small-dollar individual contributions [6]. She sits on the Financial Services Committee, which oversees Wall Street regulation, and the Oversight Committee, which investigates government waste — two perches that position her to advance anti-corruption and transparency reforms without the awkward entanglement of taking money from the industries she oversees.

Where Pressley diverges from maximum effectiveness is in bipartisan bridge-building. GovTrack's analysis of the 118th Congress found that she joined bipartisan bills less often than any other House Democrat [7]. In a legislature where nothing passes without cross-aisle votes, this is a limitation that matters. Her 2025 introduction of the bipartisan HEALTH for MOM Act with Republican Zach Nunn suggests she is capable of reaching across the aisle when the issue demands it [8], but these moments remain the exception rather than the rule.

The bottom line: Pressley is one of the clearest allies the 80% Bill has in the House. She supports the policies, refuses the money that would compromise her, and sits on committees where these fights play out. The question is not whether she believes in the agenda — it is whether she can build the coalitions to pass it.

Brittany Pettersen is the U.S. Representative for Colorado's 7th Congressional District, a Democrat elected in 2022 as the first woman to hold the seat. Born and raised in Jefferson County to a construction contractor father and a hairdresser mother, Pettersen became the first in her family to graduate high school and college, waiting tables to put herself through Metropolitan State University of Denver [1]. Her political origin story is intensely personal: her mother was prescribed a dangerous amount of opioids when Pettersen was six years old, spiraling into a thirty-year addiction to pills, alcohol, and heroin before finally entering recovery [2]. That experience propelled a decade in the Colorado state legislature and now shapes her congressional identity, where she sits on the House Financial Services Committee and the Bipartisan Addiction and Mental Health Task Force [3][4].

On the 80% Bill's agenda, Pettersen checks several important boxes. She co-sponsored the Restore Trust in Congress Act and signed the bipartisan discharge petition to ban stock trading by members of Congress, their spouses, and dependents [5]. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation and a corporate minimum tax [6]. She voted to cap the cost of insulin and reduce prescription drug prices [7]. She voted 427-1 with the House to release the Epstein files, issuing a pointed statement that "Trump could release these files TODAY if he chose to" [8]. She co-sponsored the John R. Lewis Voting Rights Advancement Act and has fought House Republicans who tried to restrict voting access in Colorado [9]. And she has been a vocal defender of Social Security, hosting roundtables and championing the Social Security Fairness Act to protect the program from cuts [10].

But the money tells a more complicated story. Pettersen is a co-founder of the Bipartisan Congressional Real Estate Caucus, launched in 2024 alongside three other members to serve as "a forum for members of Congress and real estate professionals to discuss federal policy" [11]. The Revolving Door Project flagged this caucus as "corporate landlords' new favorite," noting that Pettersen received a combined $56,500 in PAC contributions from real estate industry supporters in just two years, with the National Association of Realtors tied as the single biggest contributor to her leadership PAC [12]. Her campaign committee has received at least $18,000 in individual contributions from Apollo Global Management, the private equity giant with massive real estate holdings [12]. According to her 2023 financial disclosure, Pettersen herself jointly owns a single-family rental property in Lakewood valued at $1 million that generated $15,000 in rental income [12]. That is a striking portfolio for a representative whose constituents are struggling with housing affordability -- and it sits awkwardly next to any conversation about banning corporate purchases of single-family homes.

Pettersen's record is a study in contradictions that are common enough in Congress to be almost invisible. She is a genuinely compelling advocate on opioid recovery, voting rights, and Social Security. She backs the stock trading ban and drug price reform that supermajorities of Americans demand. But she co-founded a caucus that the real estate lobby applauded, collects five-figure checks from a private equity firm that profits from the housing crisis, and personally earns rental income from the very type of property that the 80% Bill would shield from corporate buyers. The question the 80% Bill poses is simple: whose side are you on? Pettersen's votes say one thing. Her Financial Services Committee seat, her real estate caucus, and her donor base whisper something else.

Nellie Pou has spent nearly three decades perfecting the art of inheriting a seat. In 1997, when Bill Pascrell left the New Jersey Assembly for Congress, Pou was sworn in as his replacement in the 35th district -- the first woman and first Hispanic to hold the seat [1]. Then in 2024, when Pascrell died, Pou inherited his congressional seat too, nominated by Passaic County Democratic committee members in a closed-door process that never involved a primary election [2]. If loyalty is a currency, Pou has been making deposits for a very long time.

Her 27-year tenure in Trenton was defined by quiet committee work and steady party loyalty. She rose to Senate Majority Caucus Chair, chaired the Commerce Committee, and sat on the Judiciary and Budget committees [3]. None of this generated many headlines, which was rather the point. In the state legislature, she compiled a respectable environmental record, earning endorsements from the League of Conservation Voters [4]. She arrived in Washington in January 2025 and immediately secured spots on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee and the Homeland Security Committee.

Her first year in Congress has been anything but quiet. The district she represents -- NJ-9, anchored in Paterson and Clifton -- swung dramatically rightward in 2024, narrowly voting for Donald Trump after backing Biden by 20 points in 2020 [5]. Pou won with just 50.6% of the vote [2]. Now she faces fire from both flanks. Progressives were furious when she took an AIPAC-funded trip to Israel in August 2025, particularly since her district is home to one of the largest Palestinian communities in the country and Paterson was simultaneously dealing with a water crisis that left 200,000 residents with little to no water pressure [6]. CAIR-NJ held a press conference outside her office to condemn the trip [7]. Her campaign manager called it a "fact-finding mission" where she met with both Israeli and Palestinian Authority officials [8].

Republicans, meanwhile, have attacked her votes against GOP spending bills and immigration measures [8]. It is the classic Democratic moderate squeeze: too conservative for the base, too liberal for the district. Whether Pou can survive a 2026 rematch in a Trump-leaning seat may depend less on her policy positions than on the same institutional muscle that has propelled her career -- the party machinery of North Jersey.

Jimmy Patronis is a Republican representing Florida's 1st Congressional District, the deep-red Panhandle seat vacated by Matt Gaetz after his ill-fated attorney general nomination. A fourth-generation Floridian from Panama City whose family has run the landmark Captain Anderson's seafood restaurant since 1967, Patronis spent nearly a decade in the Florida House before stints on the Public Service Commission and then eight years as the state's Chief Financial Officer [1]. Trump endorsed him before he even officially entered the race, and Patronis dispatched Democrat Gay Valimont with 57 percent in the April 2025 special election [2]. He now sits on the Small Business and Transportation and Infrastructure committees [3], arriving in Washington with the breezy confidence of a man who has never occupied a government job that wasn't handed to him by a governor or a president.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Patronis's brief congressional tenure offers limited voting data but a lengthy state record that telegraphs his likely positions. As Florida's CFO, he was a central figure in the Republican anti-ESG crusade, announcing a $2 billion divestment from BlackRock in coordination with the State Financial Officers Foundation, a group backed by fossil fuel interests and the Heritage Foundation [4]. He owned shares of Exxon Mobil and Chevron while leading that charge [4], a detail that suggests his objection to "woke" investing was not entirely ideological. On auditing the Pentagon, taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning corporate PACs, closing the carried interest loophole, or enacting paid family leave, Patronis has offered no public support — positions entirely consistent with the district that sent Gaetz to Congress for eight years. He has not co-sponsored legislation on stock trading bans, dark money disclosure, or the DISCLOSE Act, nor shown interest in Medicare drug negotiation or funding Social Security beyond the standard Republican talking point of protecting benefits without explaining how.

Where Patronis truly distinguishes himself is in the category of follow-the-money contradictions. As CFO, his political committee Treasure Florida collected contributions from an extraordinary roster of industry donors: Associated Industries of Florida gave $85,000, Florida Power & Light and parent company NextEra Energy contributed $60,000, Tampa Electric chipped in $50,000, health care management giant Centene donated $40,000, and a parade of insurance companies filled the coffers [5]. More troubling, he accepted tens of thousands in contributions from Simple Health and Health Benefits One — two Florida-based insurance companies that were legally accused of fraud — taking $35,000 from Simple Health in December 2017 and $15,000 more in May 2018 [6]. In 2019, Florida's top financial regulator Ronald Rubin filed a lawsuit accusing Patronis and a lobbyist of orchestrating a pay-to-play scheme to solicit $1 million in campaign contributions from Rubin's father in exchange for keeping Rubin in his position [6]. For his congressional race, Patronis raised nearly $3 million [7], though the full donor breakdown for that cycle has yet to be fully reported.

Patronis is, in essence, the insurance industry's man in Congress, arriving from a CFO tenure where virtually every step he took appeared to be in lockstep with whatever Florida's insurance lobby demanded [8] — even as the state's property insurance market collapsed around ordinary homeowners. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives will champion reforms supported by supermajorities of Americans; Patronis's career suggests he will champion whatever is supported by supermajorities of his donors. He left Tallahassee just as the insurance mess he helped steward became impossible to ignore, trading state headaches for a congressional seat where accountability is even harder to pin down. The restaurant may be a family tradition, but in politics, Patronis serves an entirely different clientele.
